title: Modelling monthly data
text: Time series data often collected monthly counts, example, monthly number deaths hospital admissions. vignette use monthly number deaths cardiovascular disease (CVD) people aged 75 Los-Angeles years 1987 2000 (n=168n=168 observations). compare three models examining seasonal pattern using month. First load data use ggplot2 draw lineplot monthly death counts time.  clear seasonal pattern, deaths winter months fewer summer.
code: ggplot(CVD, aes(x = yrmon, y = cvd)) +   geom_line(colour = 'darkred', linewidth = 1.05) +   labs(     x = "Time",     y = "Monthly number of CVD deaths"   ) +   theme_bw()

title: Modelling monthly data
text: first model, treat month separate category fixed parameter.  outcome counts, best use Poisson distribution. Using family = quasipoisson instead family = poisson accounts -dispersion data, variance counts greater mean. Without acknowledging -dispersion, confidence intervals estimated effects narrow. can see change quasipoisson poisson. adjust population size using offset scaled 100,000 people. useful adjusting changes time population size. also adjust varying number days month using offsetmonth=TRUE option longer months deaths average shorter months (e.g., January vs February). estimates plot rate ratios. used June reference month, rate ratio month 1. plot shows typical seasonal pattern winter increase deaths peaks January (around 40% higher June).
code: model1 <- monthglm(   formula = cvd ~ 1,   data = CVD,   family = quasipoisson(),   offsetpop = expression(pop / 100000),   offsetmonth = TRUE,   refmonth = 6 ) plot(model1, ylab = 'Rate ratio') #> Warning: `plot.monthglm()` was deprecated in season 0.3.17. #> Use `autoplot()` for a ggplot object you can extend: #> ℹ  autoplot(x) + ggplot2::labs(x = ..., y = ...) #> This warning is displayed once per session. #> Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was #> generated.

title: Modelling monthly data
text: Next fit month random instead fixed effect. allows monthly estimates follow distribution. distribution often used random effects Normal distribution. fit random effects model switch Bayesian approach using nimble package. , assume counts follow Poisson distribution. two offsets, one population one days month. month fitted random effect centred zero. ensure month effects sum zero, create centred version subtracting mean. Next set data ready nimble use. number days month scaled 30 day month. Next create initial values get chains started, using estimate mean intercept. Next run Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) estimates using two chains. plot samples chains intercept quick check estimates converged.  Next plot estimated month effect 95% credible intervals.  approach can described `global smooth’ applied shrinkage factor months equally using Normal distribution.
code: code_random_month <- nimbleCode({   ## Likelihood   for (i in 1:N) {     # loop through time     cvd[i] ~ dpois(mu[i])     log(mu[i]) <- log(offset[i]) + log(n_days_month[i]) + alpha + beta.c[month[i]]   }   # priors   alpha ~ dnorm(0, sd = 100)   for (k in 1:12) {     beta[k] ~ dnorm(0, tau = tau.month)     beta.c[k] <- beta[k] - mean.beta   }   mean.beta <- mean(beta[1:12])   tau.month ~ dgamma(1, 1)   # rescale to standard deviation   sigma.month <- 1 / sqrt(tau.month)  }) constants <- list(   N = nrow(CVD),   month = CVD$month,   # scaled to per 100,000 people   offset = CVD$pop / 100000,   # scaled to per 30 days   n_days_month = (CVD$n_days_month) / 30 ) data <- list(cvd = CVD$cvd) inits <- list(   tau.month = 1,   alpha = log(mean(CVD$cvd / (CVD$pop / 100000))),   beta = rep(0, 12) ) # parameters to store parms <- c('alpha', 'beta.c', 'sigma.month') # define the model model <- nimbleModel(   code_random_month,   data = data,   inits = inits,   constants = constants ) #> Defining model #> Building model #> Setting data and initial values #> Running calculate on model #>   [Note] Any error reports that follow may simply reflect missing values in model variables. #> Checking model sizes and dimensions #>   [Note] This model is not fully initialized. This is not an error. #>          To see which variables are not initialized, use model$initializeInfo(). #>          For more information on model initialization, see help(modelInitialization). # MCMC samples thin <- 3 MCMC <- 1000 mcmc_random <- nimbleMCMC(   model = model,   inits = inits,   monitors = parms,   # times 2 for burn-in   niter = MCMC * 2 * thin,    thin = thin,   nchains = 2,   nburnin = MCMC,   summary = TRUE,   # one seed per chain   setSeed = c(1, 2),    WAIC = TRUE ) #> Compiling #>   [Note] This may take a minute. #>   [Note] Use 'showCompilerOutput = TRUE' to see C++ title: Modelling monthly data
text: previous global smooth likely best smoothing approach know neighbouring months common distant months. can model correlation using neighbouring approach often used spatial models. First, create neighbourhood matrix 12 months. 12 12 matrix, month two neighbours. createAdj function create adjacency matrix needed spatial CAR function, CAR stands conditional autoregressive. case autoregression applied neighbouring months create smooth. data needs updated neighbourhood information.   plots rate ratios two models similar. can compare fit two models using WAIC. model parameters WAIC Random   Neighbours   neighbourhood model uses slightly parameters extra complexity improved fit. strong seasonal pattern meant neighbouring smoothing needed. Note, simplicity, examples include -dispersion Bayesian models.

Likelihood   for (i in 1:N) {     # loop through time     cvd[i] ~ dpois(mu[i])     log(mu[i]) <- log(offset[i]) + log(n_days_month[i]) + alpha + beta[month[i]]   }   # priors   alpha ~ dnorm(0, sd = 100)   beta[1:12] ~ dcar_normal(     adj[1:L],     weights[1:L],     num[1:12],     tau.neighbours,     zero_mean = 1   )   tau.month ~ dgamma(1, 1)   tau.neighbours ~ dgamma(1, 1)   # rescale to standard deviation   sigma.month <- 1 / sqrt(tau.month)    sigma.neighbours <- 1 / sqrt(tau.neighbours) }) constants <- list(   N = nrow(CVD),   L = length(adj$adj),   adj = adj$adj,   num = adj$num,   weights = adj$weight,   month = CVD$month,   # scaled to per 100,000 people   offset = CVD$pop / 100000,    # scaled to per 30 days   n_days_month = (CVD$n_days_month) / 30 ) # parameters to store parms <- c('alpha', 'beta', 'sigma.month', 'sigma.neighbours')  # define the model model <- nimbleModel(   code_neighbour_month,   data = data,   inits = inits,   constants = constants ) #> Defining model #> Building model #> Setting data and initial values #> Running calculate on model #>   [Note] Any error reports that follow may simply reflect missing values in model variables. #> Checking model sizes and dimensions #>   [Note] This model is not fully initialized. text: use data daily number deaths cardiovascular disease (CVD) people aged 75 Los Angeles years 1987 2000. load data use ggplot2 draw boxplot daily death counts month.  clear seasonal pattern, deaths winter months fewer summer. ’s also evidence greater variance winter months, expect count process, variance proportional mean.
code: data(CVDdaily) ggplot(   data = CVDdaily,   aes(     x = factor(month),     y = cvd   ) ) +   geom_boxplot() +   labs(     x = "Month",     y = "Daily number of CVD deaths"   ) +   theme_bw()

title: Season tutorial
text: Deaths increase many countries around world temperature outside optimal range, optimal range varying climate. plot shows daily death counts daily temperatures. Increases deaths apparent low high temperatures, suggesting non-linear association temperature cardiovascular deaths.
code: ggplot(   CVDdaily,   aes(     x = tmpd,     y = cvd   ) ) +   geom_point() +   labs(     x = "Temperature (degrees F)",     y = "Daily number of CVD deaths"   ) +   theme_bw()

title: Season tutorial
text: now examine association temperature death using case-crossover model. model compares number deaths case control days, uses controls near case day. choosing control days near case days, model controls long-term trends seasonal patterns. use default cases controls selected 28 day (4 week) windows. model fitted using conditional logistic regression. technical details book Analysing Seasonal Health Data. model non-linear effect temperature, first create spline temperature knots 60 75 degrees Fahrenheit, essentially means expect change association around temperatures. Deaths due temperature can occur days exposure. example, person heart attack hot day, admitted hospital alive, dies hospital days later. account include lag 14 days. using lagged temperature lose observations start data, temperature data year 1986. use [dlnm](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/dlnm/index.html) library create non-linear lagged spline basis. include categorical variable day week, evidence deaths vary day week. model takes short run. large study just 230,000 deaths 5,000 case days. coefficients log odds ratios. elsewhere vignette, estimates quoted many decimal places, presented paper recommend using guidelines. can see deaths Monday compared reference day Sunday. temperature estimates hard interpret best shown reconstructing nine spline estimates plot.
code: # make a spline basis that has a lag and is non-linear tmpd.basis <- crossbasis(   CVDdaily$tmpd,   # 14 day lag   lag = 14,   # 3 degrees of freedom for lag; ns = natural spline   arglag = list(fun = "ns", df = 3),   # knots at 65 and 75 degrees   argvar = list(fun = "ns", knots = c(60, 75)) ) # add the spline basis variables to the data CVDdaily <- cbind(CVDdaily, tmpd.basis[seq_len(nrow(CVDdaily)), ]) # create the regression formula spline.names <- colnames(tmpd.basis) formula <- paste(   "cvd ~",   paste(spline.names, collapse = " + "),   "+ Mon + Tue + Wed + Thu + Fri + Sat" ) model <- casecross(stats::as.formula(formula), data = CVDdaily) summary(model) #> Time-stratified case-crossover with a stratum length of 28 days #> Total number of cases 229759  #> Number of case days with available control days 5098  #> Average number of control days per case day 23.2  #>  #> Parameter Estimates: #>               coef exp(coef)    se(coef)          z     Pr(>|z|) #> v1.l1 -0.020874306 0.9793421 0.011889922 -1.7556303 7.915156e-02 #> v1.l2 -0.066362396 0.9357917 0.008656561 -7.6661383 1.772520e-14 #> v1.l3  0.002178049 1.0021804 0.008944090  0.2435182 8.076040e-01 #> v2.l1 -0.191012092 0.8261226 0.038352025 -4.9804957 6.342163e-07 #> v2.l2  0.014749261 1.0148586 0.029740115  0.4959383 6.199380e-01 #> v2.l3 -0.014302130 0.9857997 0.029749686 -0.4807490 6.306949e-01 #> v3.l1 -0.150126292 0.8605993 0.023333994 -6.4338019 1.244511e-10 #> v3.l2  0.117420420 1.1245921 0.017741751  6.6183107 3.633268e-11 #> v3.l3 -0.058042887 0.9436095 0.018876807 -3.0748254 2.106258e-03 #> Mon    0.036431253 1.0371030 0.007820245  4.6585818 3.183953e-06 #> Tue    0.018269159 1.0184371 0.007841133  2.3299131 1.981074e-02 #> Wed   -0.011365776 0.9886986 0.007868064 -1.4445454 1.485856e-01 #> Thu   -0.008998068 0.9910423 0.007856868 -1.1452488 2.521061e-01 #> Fri    0.009599729 1.0096460 0.007855806  1.2219916 2.217108e-01 #> Sat    0.014931771 1.0150438 0.007861585  1.8993336 5.752063e-02

what: Plot of the non-linear association between temperature and death
title: Season tutorial
text: use coefficients variance–covariance matrix reconstruct three-dimensional plot lag temperature. examine temperatures range 45 85 degrees Fahrenheit. estimates centred using reference temperature 70 degrees.  dominant feature large spike deaths high temperatures day exposure (lag day zero).
code: # extract the coefficients and variance--covariance matrix for the spline terms coef <- coefficients(model$cox_model) index <- names(coef) %in% spline.names coef <- coef[index] vcov <- vcov(model$cox_model)[index, index] for.plot <- crosspred(   basis = tmpd.basis,   coef = coef,   vcov = vcov,   at = seq(45, 85, 1),   model.link = "log",   cen = 70 ) par(mai = c(0.2, 0, 0, 0)) # reduce plot margins plot(   for.plot,   xlab = "Temperature (degrees F)",   zlab = "Odds ratio",   ylab = "Lag (days)" )

what: Plot of the temperature and death association averaging over all lags
title: Season tutorial
text: Another useful plot overall change risk summarises results across lags. plot shows mean odds (solid line) 95% confidence interval (shaded area). first put estimates data.frame can used ggplot2.
code: to.plot <- data.frame(   temperature = for.plot$predvar,   mean = for.plot$allRRfit,   lower = for.plot$allRRlow,   upper = for.plot$allRRhigh ) ggplot(   data = to.plot,   aes(     x = temperature,     y = mean,     ymin = lower,     ymax = upper   ) ) +   geom_hline(     lty = 2,     yintercept = 1   ) + # horizontal reference line at no change in odds   geom_ribbon(alpha = 0.3) +   geom_line() +   labs(     x = "Temperature (degrees F)",     y = "Odds ratio"   ) +   theme_bw()

code: for.plot <- crosspred(   basis = tmpd.basis,   coef = coef,   vcov = vcov,   at = 80,   lag = c(0, 14),   model.link = "log",   cen = 70 ) to.plot <- data.frame(   lag = for.plot$lag[1]:for.plot$lag[2],   mean = as.numeric(for.plot$matRRfit),   lower = as.numeric(for.plot$matRRlow),   upper = as.numeric(for.plot$matRRhigh) ) ggplot(   data = to.plot,   aes(     x = lag,     y = mean,     ymin = lower,     ymax = upper   ) ) +   geom_hline(     lty = 2,     yintercept = 1   ) + # horizontal reference line at no change in odds   geom_ribbon(alpha = 0.3) +   geom_line() +   labs(     x = "Lag (days)",     y = "Odds ratio"   ) +   theme_bw()

title: Season tutorial
text: shown second plot vignette, seasonal pattern appeared vary year--year, larger peaks years. non-stationary seasonal pattern. can model pattern splitting time series trend, seasonal pattern(s) residuals. Details method available paper: Estimating trends seasonality coronary heart disease. use monthly data rather daily data primarily interested seasonal patterns, daily data take much longer run. use response variable adj adjusted monthly counts CVD deaths accounts differences month lengths (e.g., 28 29 days February 31 January). model takes minutes run uses Markov chain Monte Carlo samples estimate model parameters.  estimated mean amplitude 207, average 207 extra deaths per month seasonal winter peak compared year-round average. 95% confidence interval peak 182 232 monthly deaths. plot shows long-term non-linear trend non-stationary seasonal pattern. seasonal peaks higher years, including 1989 2000, matches plot data.
code: set.seed(1234) # set the random seed to give repeatable results f <- 12 # a single twelve month cycle tau <- c(10, 50) # achieved via trial-and-error; small tau -> less variability ns.season <- nscosinor(   data = CVD,   response = "adj",   cycles = f,   niters = 2000,   burnin = 500,   tau = tau,   div = 1000 ) #> Iteration number 1000 of 2000 Iteration number 2000 of 2000  summary(ns.season) #> Statistics for non-stationary cosinor based on MCMC chains #> Number of MCMC samples = 1501 #>  #> Standard deviations #> Residual, mean=113.402, 95% CI [100.492, 127.221] #> Cycle=12 #> Season, mean=0.157644, 95% CI [0.0446635, 0.319579] #>  #> Phase and amplitude #> Cycle=12 #> Amplitude, mean=205.995, 95% CI [180.267, 233.145] #> Phase (radians), mean=0.69756, 95% CI [0.579905, 0.829052] plot(ns.season)

what: Testing for non-linearity in time series
title: Season tutorial
text: Another useful function test non-linearity time series using third-order moment. non-linear extension familiar second-order tests linearity, autocorrelation function. check remaining non-linearity time series residuals non-stationary model seasonal pattern monthly CVD deaths. check lag 12 months. evidence remaining non-linearity residuals lags 2 4 months.  plot third order moment shows lags strongest non-linear interactions (0,2), (4,4) (10,10).
code: set.seed(1234) # set the random seed to give repeatable results ntest.residuals <- nonlintest(   ns.season$residuals,   n.lag = 12,   n.boot = 500 ) ntest.residuals #> Largest and smallest co-ordinates of the third-order moment outside the test limits #> Largest positive difference at lags: #> 4 4  #> Size of largest largest difference: #> 402602.8  #> Largest negative difference at lags: #> 0 2  #> Size of largest negative difference: #> -689838.6  #>  #> Bootstrap test of non-linearity using the third-order moment #> Statistics for areas outside test limits: #> observed     obs/SD     median-null    95%-null    p-value #> 2961568 7.056636 283627.3 1253176 0 plot <- plot(   ntest.residuals,   plot = FALSE ) #> Warning: `plot.nonlintest()` was deprecated in season 0.3.17. #> Use `autoplot()` for a ggplot object you can extend: #> ℹ  autoplot(x) + ggplot2::theme_bw() #> This warning is displayed once per session. #> Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was #> generated. #> Warning: The `plot` argument of `plot.nonlintest()` is deprecated as of season 0.3.17. #> ℹ Assign the return value of `autoplot(x)` instead. #> This warning is displayed once per session. #> Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was #> generated. plot +   scale_x_continuous(breaks = 0:12) +   scale_y_continuous(breaks = 0:12) +   theme_bw() +   labs(     x = "Lag 1 (months)",     y = "Lag 2 (months)"   ) +   theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

title: Case–crossover Analysis to Control for Seasonality — casecross
text: formula formula. dependent variable integer count (e.g., daily number deaths). data data set data frame. exclusion exclusion period (days) around cases, set 2 (default). Must greater equal zero smaller stratalength. stratalength length stratum days, set 28 (default). matchdow match case control days using day week (TRUE/default=FALSE). matching addition strata matching. usefinalwindow use last stratum time series, likely contain less days strata (TRUE/default=FALSE). matchconf match case control days using important confounder (optional; must quotes). matchconf variable match . matching addition strata matching. Default NULL - confounder used. confrange range confounder within case control days treated match (optional). Range = matchconf (case day) \(+/-\) confrange. stratamonth use strata based months, default=FALSE. Instead fixed strata size using stratalength. date_col Character. column name date variable. Default "date".

title: Cosinor Regression Model for Detecting Seasonality in Yearly Data or Circadian Patterns in Hourly Data — cosinor
text: cosinor model captures seasonal pattern using sinusoid. therefore suitable relatively simple seasonal patterns symmetric stationary. default fit annual seasonal pattern (cycle=1), higher frequencies possible (e.g., twice per year: cycle=2). model fitted using sine cosine term together describe sinusoid. parameters added generalized linear model, model can fitted range dependent data (e.g., Normal, Poisson, Binomial). Unlike nscosinor() model, cosinor model can applied unequally spaced data.

what: Test of Non-linearity of a Time Series — nonlintest
title: Test of Non-linearity of a Time Series — nonlintest
text: bootstrap test non-linearity time series using third-order moment.

title: Test of Non-linearity of a Time Series — nonlintest
code: nonlintest(data, n.lag, n.boot, alpha = 0.05)

title: Test of Non-linearity of a Time Series — nonlintest
text: data vector equally spaced numeric observations (time series). n.lag number lags tested using third-order moment, maximum = length time series. n.boot number bootstrap replications (suggested minimum 100; 1000 better). alpha statistical significance level test (default=0.05).

title: Test of Non-linearity of a Time Series — nonlintest
text: Returns object class "nonlintest" following parts: region: region third order moment test exceeds limits (n.lag). n.lag: maximum lag tested using third-order moment. stats: list statistics area outside test limits: outside: total area outside limits (summed whole third-order moment). stan: total area outside limits divided standard deviation give standardised estimate. median: median area outside test limits. upper: (1-alpha)th percentile area outside limits. pvalue: bootstrap p-value area outside limits test series linear. test: reject null hypothesis series linear (TRUE/FALSE).

title: Test of Non-linearity of a Time Series — nonlintest
text: test uses aaft create linear surrogates second-order properties, (third-order) non-linearity. third-order moments (third) linear surrogates actual series compared lags 0 n.lag (excluding skew co-ordinates (0,0)). bootstrap test works overall area outside limits, gives indication overall non-linearity. plot using region shows co-ordinates third order moment exceed null hypothesis limits, can useful clue guessing type non-linearity. example, large value co-ordinates (0,1) might caused bi-linear series \(X_t=\alpha X_{t-1}\varepsilon_{t-1} +\varepsilon_t\).

title: Test of Non-linearity of a Time Series — nonlintest
text: Barnett AG & Wolff RC (2005) Time-Domain Test Types Nonlinearity, IEEE Transactions Signal Processing, vol 53, pages 26–33 doi: 10.1109/TSP.2004.838942 .

title: Non-stationary Cosinor — nscosinor
text: Decompose time series using non-stationary cosinor seasonal pattern.

title: Non-stationary Cosinor — nscosinor
code: nscosinor(   data,   response,   cycles,   niters = 1000,   burnin = 500,   tau,   lambda = 1/12,   div = 50,   monthly = TRUE,   alpha = 0.05,   year_col = "year",   month_col = "month" )

title: Non-stationary Cosinor — nscosinor
text: data Data frame. Assumes missing data. Year month columns default assumed "year", "month", respectively. can specified arguments year_col month_col. response character. Response variable. cycles vector cycles units time, e.g., six twelve month pattern cycles=c(6,12). niters total number MCMC samples (default=1000). burnin number MCMC samples discarded burn-(default=500). tau vector smoothing parameters, tau[1] trend, tau[2] 1st seasonal parameter, tau[3] 2nd seasonal parameter, etc. Larger values tau allow change observations hence greater potential flexibility trend season. lambda distance observations (lambda=1/12 monthly data, default). div divisor MCMC sample progress reported (default=50). monthly TRUE monthly data. alpha Statistical significance level used confidence intervals. year_col character. column referring year. Default "year". month_col character. column referring month. Default "month".

title: Non-stationary Cosinor — nscosinor
text: Returns object class "nsCosinor" following parts: call: original call nscosinor function. time: year month monthly data. trend: mean trend 95\ season: mean season(s) 95\ oseason: overall season(s) 95\ season one seasonal cycle. fitted: fitted values 95\ season(s). residuals: residuals based mean trend season(s). n: length series. chains: MCMC chains (class mcmc) variance estimates: standard error overall noise (std.error), standard error season(s) (std.season), phase(s) amplitude(s). cycles: vector cycles units time.

title: Non-stationary Cosinor — nscosinor
text: model designed decompose equally spaced time series trend, season(s) noise. seasonal estimate estimated \(s_t=A_t\cos(\omega_t-P_t)\), t time, \(A_t\) non-stationary amplitude, \(P_t\) non-stationary phase \(\omega_t\) frequency. non-stationary seasonal pattern one changes time, hence model gives potentially flexible seasonal estimates. frequency seasonal estimate(s) controlled cycle. cycles specified units time. data monthly, setting lambda=1/12 cycles=12 fit annual seasonal pattern. data daily, setting lambda= 1/365.25 cycles=365.25 fit annual seasonal pattern. Specifying cycles= c(182.6,365.25) fit two seasonal patterns, one twice-annual cycle, one annual cycle. estimates made using forward backward sweep Kalman filter. Repeated estimates made using Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC). reason model can take long time run. give stable estimates reasonably long sample used (niters), possibly poor initial estimates discarded (burnin).

title: Non-stationary Cosinor — nscosinor
text: Barnett, .G., Dobson, .J. (2010) Analysing Seasonal Health Data. Springer. doi:10.1007/978-3-642-10748-1 Barnett, .G., Dobson, .J. (2004) Estimating trends seasonality coronary heart disease Statistics Medicine. 23(22) 3505–23.

text: Adrian Barnett .barnett@qut.edu.au

title: Print and summary methods for a Cosinor — summary.Cosinor
text: Print summary methods Cosinor object produced cosinor(). summary() summarises sinusoidal seasonal pattern tests whether statistically significant seasonal circadian pattern (assuming smooth sinusoidal pattern). amplitude describes average height sinusoid, phase describes location peak. scale amplitude depends link function: logistic regression amplitude given probability scale; Poisson regression amplitude given absolute scale. print() uses glm method print() underlying model.

title: Print and summary methods for a Cosinor — summary.Cosinor
text: summary.Cosinor() returns list following named elements: n: sample size. amp: estimated amplitude. amp.scale: scale estimated amplitude (empty standard regression; "probability scale" logistic regression; "absolute scale" Poisson regression). phase: estimated peak phase time scale. lphase: estimated low phase time scale (half year /phase). significant: statistically significant sinusoid (TRUE/FALSE). alpha: statistical significance level. digits: minimal number significant digits. text: add explanatory text returned phase value (TRUE) return number (FALSE). type: type data (yearly/monthly/weekly/hourly). ctable: table regression coefficients. print.Cosinor() print.summary.Cosinor() called side effect printing console invisibly return x. summary output cosinor().

 165 Residual #> Null Deviance:	    4330  #> Residual Deviance: 1496 	AIC: 3023 summary(res) #> Cosinor test: #> Number of observations = 168  #> Amplitude = 232.34 (absolute scale)  #> Phase: Month = 1.3  #> Low point: Month = 7.3  #> Significant seasonality based on adjusted significance level of 0.025  =  TRUE  #>  #> Regression coefficients: #>               Estimate  Std..Error     z.value     Pr...z.. #> (Intercept) 7.21933179 0.002093436 3448.557005 0.000000e+00 #> cosw        0.15552367 0.002955997   52.612939 0.000000e+00 #> sinw        0.02237303 0.002949022    7.586594 3.284251e-14 # } # \donttest{ ## cardiovascular disease data (offset based on number of days in... ## ...the month scaled to an average month length) res <- cosinor(   cvd ~ 1,   date = 'month',   data = CVD,   type = 'monthly',   family = poisson(),   offsetmonth = TRUE   ) res #>  #> Call:  stats::glm(formula = form, family = family, data = data, offset = offset) #>  #> Coefficients: #> (Intercept)         cosw         sinw   #>     7.21933      0.15552      0.02237   #>  #> Degrees of Freedom: 167 Total (i.e. Null); text: list following elements: cycles: vector cycles units time, e.g., six twelve month pattern cycles=c(6,12). niters: total number MCMC samples. burnin: number MCMC samples discarded burn-. tau: vector smoothing parameters, tau[1] trend, tau[2] 1st seasonal parameter, tau[3] 2nd seasonal parameter, etc. stats: summary statistics (mean confidence interval) residual standard deviation, standard deviation seasonal cycle, amplitude phase cycle.

title: Summary for a Non-stationary Cosinor — summary.nsCosinor
text: amplitude describes average height seasonal cycle, phase describes location peak. results phase given radians (0 2\(\pi\)), can transformed time scale using invyrfraction() making sure first divide 2\(\pi\). larger standard deviation seasonal cycles, greater non-stationarity. larger standard deviation means change time.

text: New vignette, “Modelling monthly data”, demonstrates use createAdj() nimble R package. autoplot() (ggplot version base R’s plot method) methods added peri, third, Cosinor, monthglm, Monthmean, nsCosinor, nonlintest classes. returns ggplot object can extended + theme_bw(), + labs(), etc. (#42, #33). New plot_circle() plot_month() functions return ggplot objects (#42). added broom tidiers: tidy(), augment(), glance() (#39) Made ciPhase() return data.frame rather list() (#43) Made invyrfraction_chr() invyrfraction_num() avoid specifying text = TRUE/FALSE, make function use explicit, (#43) Made peri() return list columns peri, freq_radians (formerly f) freq_cycles made sinusoid() return tibble(), made autoplot method removed cat() print wtest() return tibble list() nscosinor, now optionally specify year month column name, don’t need columns specifically named “year” “month” - “yr”, “mn” even. #44 casecross, now optionally specify date column name, don’t need columns specifically named “date”. #44
